Don't let the old man beard fool you—Kinsaishi packs enough voltage to fry a fish. But does this 3-star Ninja still have the spark in today's HP-heavy meta, or is he just static noise? We break down the stats.
Kinsaishi: The Thunderous Monk Who Shocked the System
Kinsaishi: The Thunderous Monk - Chain Lightning in a Bottle?

Kinsaishi entered the arena as part of the Ninja Tower expansion, bringing the versatile "Charge" mana mechanic to the 3-star rarity. As a Holy (Yellow) Monk, he offers a flexible playstyle that can switch between rapid-fire sniping and board-wide devastation. While he lacks a costume to date, his base form remains a unique specimen in the rare tier—a glass cannon that can either save the day with a precision strike or fizzle out if a stiff breeze hits him.

Kinsaishi’s design is a fantastic blend of serene wisdom and crackling power. He’s depicted as an elder monk, traditional straw hat and prayer beads in place, but with glowing eyes and hands wreathed in untamed lightning. It’s a classic "master who looks frail but can destroy you with a pinky finger" trope, and it works beautifully. The lighting effects on the card really pop, giving him a dynamic energy that many static 3-star portraits lack.

Here is where the "glass cannon" label gets glued to his forehead. Kinsaishi’s attack stat (543 base, 761 LB2) is actually surprisingly competitive, holding its own against even some modern 2025 releases like

. However, his survivability is where the cracks show. With a base HP of 627, he is severely malnourished compared to modern 3-stars who regularly push 900-1000 HP out of the box. In today’s meta of heavy hitters and passive damage, Kinsaishi is made of wet tissue paper. He hits hard, but he cannot take a hit back.
The beauty of Kinsaishi lies in the flexibility of the Ninja Charge:
Deals 145% to the target and 2 random enemies. This is fantastic for finishing off weakened foes or breaking Divine Shields/Counterattacks early in the match without committing to a full charge.
Deals 195% to the target and 3 random enemies. This is his sweet spot, offering substantial spread damage.
Deals 220% to the target and 4 random enemies. Basically a hit-all (unless you’re fighting 5 enemies and RNG hates you).

The "Random Enemy" targeting is the double-edged sword here. Unlike a standard "Hit-3" or "Hit-All", Kinsaishi can potentially double-tap enemies if the field is thinned out, or he might tickle a tank while ignoring the sniper about to kill him.

1100% total damage at "Slow" speed is respectable for a 3-star, but modern heroes often do this with added utility (healing, mana cut, etc.). Kinsaishi provides pure, unadulterated damage with zero utility.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Ninja family bonus is one of the best in the game, even for 3-stars.

The chance to Dodge special skills or Counterattack is a lifesaver for a hero with such low HP. If you pair him with another Ninja (even a 4 or 5-star in events), that dodge chance can be the difference between Kinsaishi firing his special or being deleted.
Kinsaishi does not have specific passive skills outside of the family bonus.

is the perfect choice for him. Since he’s a damage dealer with high attack scaling, this simple boost ensures his lightning hits as hard as possible. Giving him a defensive power would be like putting a band-aid on a broken dam.

Upon release in early 2023, Kinsaishi was a breath of fresh air for the 3-star tier, introducing the charge mechanic to lower-level tournaments. He was seen as a "Mini-Cobalt" or a more reliable version of the erratic
While his damage percentages hold up, his stats—specifically HP—have aged poorly. Modern 3-stars like
(released late 2025) have nearly double his survivability while matching his offensive output.
He is no longer a "must-have" generalist. However, in
tournaments where healing is banned, his raw damage output and fast speed make him a dangerous threat. In regular play, he is too fragile to rely on consistently without heavy protection.
Kinsaishi is here for a good time, not a long time. Don't try to make him a tank.
Maximizes his damage output. Since his HP is so low, a few extra points of health won't save him from a modern sniper, but extra attack might kill them first.
He dies if you look at him wrong.
Slightly higher chance to survive a slash attack.
Wastes his primary asset (his high attack stat) for a negligible gain in survivability.
. Treat him as a guided missile. You want him to do maximum damage before he inevitably goes down.
Can fire fast to finish a target or hold for a board wipe.
Still punches above his weight class in raw damage.
Dodge chance provides RNG-based survival that stats can't match.
His HP is shockingly low by modern standards.
You can't control who the "extra" lightning bolts hit, which can be frustrating when trying to take out a specific threat.
He offers damage and nothing else—no cleanses, no buffs, no ailments.
His lack of self-sustain doesn't matter as much here, and his fast damage is premium.
His 1x charge is great for clearing early waves quickly to improve finish times.
The Monk class helps him resist ailment blocks, ensuring he can keep firing.
Pair him with other Ninjas (if allowed) or fast mana troops. The goal is to fire his 1x charge repeatedly to chip down the enemy team.
Use him alongside a "Def Down" hero like
. Fire the defense down first, then unleash Kinsaishi's 2x or 3x charge for a wipe.
Kinsaishi hits multiple enemies randomly. If he hits a riposte hero, he will instantly kill himself due to his low HP and high attack.
Since he relies on charging up, heroes that cut mana (like
) can strand him in "no man's land" between charges.
Kinsaishi is a great pull early on. His flexibility teaches you how to manage mana and timing. Don't be afraid to fire his 1x charge—you don't always need to wait for the big boom.

He is likely a depth hero for you now. He shines in 3-star tournaments where Yellow is strong, but don't prioritize Limit Breaking him (especially LB2) over modern powerhouses unless you specifically need his Monk class or Ninja family bonus for a synergy team.
The community's reception to Kinsaishi was generally positive but cautious regarding his stats.
Many players initially compared him to
"I wonder if this hero is better than Poppy? 220% at lv3 but guaranteed hits."
The consensus generally shifted to Kinsaishi being more consistent, but Poppy having higher "high-roll" potential.
The survivability issues were spotted immediately.
"Very low health, like Agnes. Usually doesn’t end well,"
warned a forum user on day one.
He consistently gets high marks for visuals.
said one player, and we have to agree.

Kinsaishi is like a firecracker: small, fragile, but capable of a surprising bang. While he has been statistically left behind by the behemoths of late 2025, his mechanics remain sound. The ability to choose between a quick jab and a haymaker is a tactical advantage that never truly goes out of style. If you can keep him alive, he'll light up the scoreboard. If you can't... well, at least he looked cool dying.
A solid tournament specialist who demands aggressive play.
